# Agentic Tooling: User Stories (Solo Operator's Perspective)

64 user stories written from the perspective of the solo, cost- and security-conscious operator who owns the whole platform — not just a session's interactive user, but the person accountable for spend and risk with no team to fall back on. Fifth in the set alongside [agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md) (the calling agent), [agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md) (the tool), [agent-tooling-user-stories-runtime-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-runtime-pov.md) (the orchestrating runtime), and [agent-tooling-user-stories-user-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-user-pov.md) (the human user of a session).

## Cost Visibility & Control

1. As the solo operator, I want a single dashboard of total spend across all providers (Anthropic, Cloudflare, etc.), so I don't have to log into five billing consoles to know my burn rate.
2. As the solo operator, I want hard spend caps enforced at the account/API-key level, not just advisory alerts, so a runaway loop can't drain my account while I'm asleep.
3. As the solo operator, I want per-project or per-key cost attribution, so I know which product is actually expensive versus which just feels expensive.
4. As the solo operator, I want anomaly detection on spend (a 10x spike in an hour), so a bug or leaked key gets caught same-day, not at end of month.
5. As the solo operator, I want idle/unused cloud resources (forgotten GPU instances, orphaned worker deployments) surfaced automatically, so I'm not paying for infrastructure I forgot to tear down.
6. As the solo operator, I want a way to simulate the cost of a workflow before running it at scale, so I don't discover the real price only after the fleet has already fanned out.
7. As the solo operator, I want free-tier and included-quota usage tracked separately from paid overage, so I know exactly when I'm about to cross into billed usage.
8. As the solo operator, I want to require explicit confirmation before any action that provisions billable infrastructure, so nothing paid gets created as an unreviewed side effect.

## Credential & Secrets Management

9. As the solo operator, I want secrets stored in one managed secret store, not scattered across `.env` files I might accidentally commit.
10. As the solo operator, I want every credential scoped to the minimum permission it needs, so a single leaked key can't cascade into full account compromise.
11. As the solo operator, I want automatic detection of secrets in diffs before they're committed or pushed, so a slip doesn't end up in git history I have to scrub later.
12. As the solo operator, I want credential rotation to be routine and low-friction, so I actually do it instead of leaving the same key alive for years out of inertia.
13. As the solo operator, I want to know immediately if a secret was exposed in a log, error message, or tool output, so I can rotate it before it's exploited.
14. As the solo operator, I want a clear inventory of every API key and its last-used date, so I can kill ones that are no longer needed instead of leaving them as standing risk.
15. As the solo operator, I want service credentials distinct from my personal ones, so a compromised automated agent can't act with my full personal authority.
16. As the solo operator, I want the option to run agents against a scoped, revocable token rather than a long-lived master credential, so I can cut off access without re-issuing everything.

## Access Control & Blast Radius

17. As the solo operator, I want a clear boundary between what an agent can do locally versus what it can do to shared/production infrastructure, since I have no second reviewer to catch an overreach.
18. As the solo operator, I want destructive infrastructure actions (deleting a database, dropping a Worker, force-pushing to main) to require a distinct, harder-to-trigger confirmation than routine file edits.
19. As the solo operator, I want a single point where I can instantly revoke all active agent sessions/tokens, so a compromised session can be shut down without hunting through every surface individually.
20. As the solo operator, I want production and staging credentials kept in physically separate stores, so a dev-environment mistake can't reach prod by accident.
21. As the solo operator, I want to know the full blast radius of a tool before granting it broad permissions, since as a solo operator I'm the only line of defense against a bad grant.
22. As the solo operator, I want network egress from agent-run code restricted by default, so a compromised dependency can't exfiltrate data to an arbitrary host.
23. As the solo operator, I want a documented "break glass" procedure for when automation misbehaves, so I'm not improvising incident response for the first time during an actual incident.
24. As the solo operator, I want multi-factor auth enforced on every account that holds billing or production access, since I have no team to fall back on if my single account is compromised.

## Monitoring & Alerting

25. As the solo operator, I want a single alerting channel — not five dashboards — for anything security- or cost-relevant, so I don't miss a signal buried in a tool I forgot to check.
26. As the solo operator, I want alerts prioritized by actual severity, so a real incident isn't lost in routine noise from low-stakes notifications.
27. As the solo operator, I want failed-login and unusual-access alerts on every account tied to the platform, since I'm the only person watching for them.
28. As the solo operator, I want a weekly automated summary of spend, security events, and anomalies, so I stay current without a daily manual review habit I might not maintain.
29. As the solo operator, I want alerting to work even when I'm not actively at a terminal (push notification, SMS, email), since solo operation means there's no on-call rotation to cover me.
30. As the solo operator, I want logs retained long enough to investigate an incident discovered late, since a solo operator often notices problems well after they started.
31. As the solo operator, I want a canary/health-check on critical services that pages me directly, rather than relying on users to report an outage.
32. As the solo operator, I want alert fatigue actively managed (tunable thresholds, snoozing), so I don't start ignoring the channel that's supposed to catch the one real problem.

## Backup, Recovery & Continuity

33. As the solo operator, I want automated, tested backups of all critical data stores, since I have no second person to catch a bad restore before it matters.
34. As the solo operator, I want backup restoration tested periodically, not just assumed to work, since an untested backup is a false sense of security.
35. As the solo operator, I want infrastructure defined as code, so I can rebuild the whole platform from scratch if a single machine or account is lost.
36. As the solo operator, I want a documented recovery runbook I can follow at 3am half-awake, since incident response can't depend on me being at my sharpest.
37. As the solo operator, I want redundancy for anything genuinely critical, but no more than that, since solo-operator budget can't afford enterprise-grade redundancy everywhere.
38. As the solo operator, I want key material (signing keys, root credentials) backed up somewhere outside the primary infrastructure, so losing one system doesn't lock me out permanently.
39. As the solo operator, I want a clear answer to "what's my actual recovery time if X dies," verified in practice, not assumed from an architecture diagram.
40. As the solo operator, I want continuity plans sized to what a single person can actually execute alone under stress, not a playbook written for a team.

## Vendor & Provider Risk Management

41. As the solo operator, I want to know my actual exposure to any single vendor's outage or pricing change, so a policy shift elsewhere doesn't blindside my whole platform.
42. As the solo operator, I want critical dependencies to have a documented fallback or exit path, so a vendor deprecating a service doesn't strand me with no migration plan.
43. As the solo operator, I want to track which vendors hold sensitive data or credentials, so a third-party breach's blast radius on me is knowable in advance, not discovered after the fact.
44. As the solo operator, I want pricing-tier changes from any dependency surfaced to me proactively, so a silent cost increase doesn't show up only on the invoice.
45. As the solo operator, I want to avoid hard lock-in to a single provider for anything I could plausibly need to migrate off of under pressure.
46. As the solo operator, I want vendor security incidents that affect my data to reach me directly (status page, security bulletin subscription), not discovered secondhand.
47. As the solo operator, I want contract/ToS changes from key vendors flagged, since I don't have legal counsel reviewing every update on my behalf.
48. As the solo operator, I want to periodically re-justify every recurring vendor cost against what it actually buys me, since subscriptions accumulate silently without a team enforcing a review cadence.

## Compliance-lite & Audit Trail

49. As the solo operator, I want an audit trail of who (which agent, which session) did what to production, since I'm the only person who can reconstruct an incident after the fact.
50. As the solo operator, I want data retention and deletion practices that are at least defensible if a user ever asks what I hold on them, even without a formal compliance program.
51. As the solo operator, I want a lightweight, honest privacy policy that matches what the platform actually does, not aspirational boilerplate I'd fail to uphold under scrutiny.
52. As the solo operator, I want to know exactly what user data flows through third-party AI providers, so I can represent that accurately if ever asked.
53. As the solo operator, I want logs of administrative actions (who changed a permission, who rotated a key) even when "who" is always me, so I have a timeline during an incident review.
54. As the solo operator, I want a minimal, honest terms-of-service and acceptable-use policy, so I have something to point to if a user's usage crosses a line.
55. As the solo operator, I want to avoid collecting data I don't have a clear use for, since unused data is pure downside risk with no offsetting benefit at my scale.
56. As the solo operator, I want an incident-disclosure plan drafted before an incident happens, since improvising a breach notice under pressure is where mistakes get made.

## Automation & Guardrails (since there's no second reviewer)

57. As the solo operator, I want policy-as-code guardrails (cost ceilings, permission scopes, deploy gates) enforced automatically, since there's no colleague to catch what automation misses.
58. As the solo operator, I want CI/CD to block a deploy that fails a security or cost check, rather than rely on me remembering to look every time.
59. As the solo operator, I want dependency vulnerabilities surfaced and triaged automatically, since I don't have a dedicated security team scanning for me.
60. As the solo operator, I want infrastructure changes to require a diff I can review quickly, so oversight scales with my limited time rather than requiring line-by-line manual audits.
61. As the solo operator, I want risky automation (auto-deploys, auto-scaling, auto-remediation) to default to notify-then-act with a delay, so I have a window to intervene before an automated fix makes things worse.
62. As the solo operator, I want a single command that shows overall platform health (cost, security posture, uptime) at a glance, since I don't have a team standup to surface it for me.
63. As the solo operator, I want guardrails to fail safe (deny by default) when their own logic is uncertain, since there's no second approver to catch a guardrail's own bug.
64. As the solo operator, I want the system to actively resist my own bad shortcuts under time pressure (skip the hook, force it through), since solo operation means the platform's discipline has to substitute for a team's.
